does: This paper evaluates machine unlearning algorithms under repeated, sequential unlearning scenarios and identifies two failure modes: knowledge erosion, where performance on retained data degrades across successive unlearning phases, and forgetting reversal, where previously forgotten samples become recognizable again. The authors propose SAFER (StAbility-preserving Forgetting with Effective Regularization), a continual unlearning framework that addresses these challenges through two mechanisms: maintaining representation stability for retained data to prevent gradual knowledge loss, and enforcing negative logit margins for forgotten data to prevent reemergence of unlearned information across unlearning rounds.
connects: This work extends the machine unlearning literature, which has focused primarily on single-round unlearning scenarios within frameworks addressing the right to be forgotten in AI systems. It builds on prior methods that remove influence of specific data points or classes while preserving general model knowledge, but identifies critical gaps when unlearning becomes a repeated process. The paper connects to broader continual learning and catastrophic forgetting literature, adapting insights about representation stability and regularization techniques to the novel problem of preventing both knowledge erosion on retained data and forgetting reversal on unlearned data across multiple sequential unlearning operations.
enables: This work enables more realistic deployment of machine unlearning in practical AI systems where privacy requests occur repeatedly over time. By addressing knowledge erosion and forgetting reversal, it establishes foundational methods for continual unlearning that could support regulatory compliance with repeated data deletion requests. The framework opens future research into scaling continual unlearning to large numbers of sequential unlearning phases, combining continual unlearning with model updates or new data ingestion, and developing theoretical guarantees for multi-phase unlearning stability. These contributions position continual unlearning as a critical capability for maintaining both privacy guarantees and model utility in long-lived AI systems.
